title: Case on Disqualification from Judicial Appointment based on Political Party Membership within Past Three Years
text:
- 209 -Case on Disqualification from Judicial Appointment based on 
Political Party Membership within Past Three Years
[2021Hun-Ma460, July 18, 2024]
In this case, the Court held unconstitutional the portion of Article 43, 
Section (1), Item 5 of the Court Organization Act concerning “a person 
for whom three years have not passed after his or her status as member 
of a political party was lost.” This item states political party membership 
within the past three years as a ground for disqualification from being 
appointed as a judicial officer. The Court reasoned that this provision 
infringes the right of a citizen to hold public office.
Background of the Case
Complainant is a lawyer who filed a registration with the Korean Bar 
Association as of 2012 to practice law. Complainant joined a political 
party on December 18, 2017 and withdrew from the party on March 15, 
2021.
Complainant participated in the legal document writing evaluation for 
judicial appointments in the criminal field in 2021 and passed the 
evaluation on April 15, 2021. During the process of drafting the “pledge 
regarding disqualification grounds” to be submitted to the National Court 
Administration on April 18, 2021, Complainant learned that having party 
membership within the past three years is a disqualification ground for 
judicial appointments.
In response, Complainant filed a constitutional complaint agains t 
a provision of the Court Organization Act that designates party 
membership within the past three years as a disqualification ground for 
judicial appointments.
- 210 -Subject Matter of Review
The subject matter of review in this case is whether a fundamental 
right of Complainant is infringed by the portion of Article 43, Section 
(1), Item 5 of the Court Organization Act (amended by Act No. 17125 
on March 24, 2020) relating to “a person for whom three years have not 
passed after his or her status as member of a political party was lost” 
(hereinafter referred to as the “Provision at Issue”). This portion provides 
for a disqualification ground for judicial appointments. The Provision at 
Issue reads as follows.
Provision at Issue
Court Organization Act (amended by Act No. 17125 on March 24, 
2020)
Article 43 (Grounds for Disqualification)
(1)None of the following persons shall be appointed as a judicial 
officer:
5.A member of a political party under Article 22 of the Political 
Parties Act, or a person for whom three years have not passed 
after his or her status as member of a political party was lost 
under the same Act; (Emphasis added .)
Summary of the Decision
The career civil service system prescribed by the Constitution is 
grounded in meritocracy and equal opportunity. Consequently, setting 
qualifications irrelevant to the capabilities required for a given public 
position may infringe the right to hold public office. However, as the 
Constitution guarantees the political neutrality of public officials under 
Article 7 and underscores the judicial independence under Article 103, 
- 211 -the right to hold public office with respect to judicial officers may be 
reasonably restricted to fulfill these constitutional imperatives.
Under current law, institutional mechanisms are already in place to 
ensure that judicial officers (the Supreme Court Chief Justice, Supreme 
Court Justices, judges) maintain political neutrality and judicial 
independence without unduly restricting the right to hold public office. 
Specifically, to safeguard political neutrality, incumbent judicial officers 
are prohibited from joining political parties or engaging in political 
activities. Violations of these rules may result in disciplinary action, 
criminal penalties, or removal from office through impeachment. 
Furthermore, to preserve judicial independence, the terms of the Supreme 
Court Chief Justice, Supreme Court Justices, and judges are guaranteed. 
They cannot be removed from office except through impeachment or a 
conviction carrying a sentence of imprisonment without labor or greater. 
Judicial officers are required to rule independently, guided by their 
conscience and in accordance with the Constitution and statutes. 
Moreover, if a judicial officer’s past experience could unfairly influence 
a specific case, this can be addressed through mandatory recusal, recusal 
upon request of a party, or withdrawal. The appellate system and 
consensus decision-making further ensure the objectivity and fairness of 
judgments, regardless of the inclinations of individual judicial officers. 
Notably, the Supreme Court Chief Justice and Supreme Court Justices 
undergo a more rigorous examination of their political neutrality than 
judges, as their appointment requires a confirmation hearing before the 
National Assembly and its subsequent consent.
Even if there is a need to regulate past party membership, restrictions 
on judicial appointments are only allowed to apply to cases where an 
individual actively engaged in political activities, such as holding a 
certain position within a party or running as a party candidate in a 
public election. The Court Organization Act already includes specific 
provisions in this regard. Nevertheless, disqualifying anyone with any 
party membership within the past three years from judicial appointments, 
- 212 -as with the Provision at Issue, goes beyond a reasonable scope for 
achieving legislative purposes. This excessively restricts the opportunity 
to assume public office, even in cases where there is no close 
connection to political neutrality or judicial independence.
Accordingly, the Provision at Issue violates the rule against excessive 
restriction and infringes the right of Complainant to hold public office.
Summary of Partial Unconstitutionality Opinion of Two Justices
Since a fair trial is founded on public trust in the judiciary, the 
legislator may, within certain limits, restrict the qualifications of judicial 
officers by statute when it is presumed that such qualifications would 
affect judicial independence and neutrality. Those who were a party 
member close to the time of their judicial appointment may not be 
entirely free from the influence of that party. Even if they are, their 
rulings could be perceived as politically biased, potentially undermining 
public confidence in the fairness of trials. Therefore, it is not necessarily 
an excessive restriction to establish a specific period before judicial 
appointment, which affords candidates for judicial officers opportunities 
to leave their political parties in advance to eliminate disqualifying 
conditions.
However, the Provision at Issue applies to judicial officers, including 
judges as well as the Supreme Court Chief Justice and Supreme Court 
Justices. Unlike the appointment process for the Chief Justice and 
Justices of the Supreme Court, the appointment process for judges is not 
politically influenced. Even if a judge’s past party membership could 
unfairly influence his or her rulings in specific cases, this can be 
addressed through the appellate system, which allows higher courts to 
review the judgments. Thus, while it may be necessary to disqualify 
individuals with a history of party membership from judicia l 
appointments to restore public trust in the judiciary, applying this 
disqualification to judges —who are neither the Supreme Court Chief 
- 213 -Justice nor a Supreme Court Justic e—cannot be considered the 
minimum necessary restriction for achieving the legislative purpose.
Therefore, the portion of the Provision at Issue concerning the 
Supreme Court Chief Justice and Supreme Court Justices does not 
violate the rule against excessive restriction. However, the portion 
concerning judges does violate this rule and infringes the right of 
Complainant to hold public office.
